title: Ayat al-Kursi: The Greatest Verse in the Quran — Meaning, Virtues & When to Recite It
excerpt: Ayat al-Kursi (Surah Al-Baqarah 2:255) is declared by the Prophet ﷺ as the greatest verse in the Quran. This guide explores its word-by-word meaning, its extraordinary descriptions of Allah, and the five specific times the Prophet ﷺ recommended reciting it.
author: QuranBookk
content:
<HadithBlock
  text="O Abu Mundhir! Do you know which verse of the Book of Allah is the greatest? I said: Allah and His Messenger know best. He said: O Abu Mundhir! Do you know which verse of the Book of Allah is the greatest? I said: Allahu la ilaha illa huwal-Hayyul-Qayyum. He struck me in the chest and said: May knowledge be pleasant for you, O Abu Mundhir!"
  narrator="Ubayy ibn Ka'b (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sahih Muslim 810"
/>

The Prophet ﷺ himself designated Ayat al-Kursi — the Verse of the Throne — as the greatest single verse in the Quran.
In 50 Arabic words, it presents the most comprehensive description of Allah found anywhere in the Quran: His living, self-subsisting nature;
His absolute knowledge; His all-encompassing sovereignty; His transcendence above His creation.

## The Verse Itself

<AyahBlock
  arabic="ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ"
  translation="Allah — there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of existence. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is before them and what will be after them, and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High, the Most Great."
  reference="Surah Al-Baqarah 2:255"
/>

## Word-by-Word: Ten Divine Attributes in 50 Words

### 1. Allahu la ilaha illa huwa — "Allah, there is no deity except Him"

The verse begins with the *shahada* — there is no god but Allah.
Before describing *what* Allah is, the verse establishes *what He is not*: not one of many, not one who shares divinity, not one whose power is contingent on anything else.
The negation before the affirmation.

### 2. Al-Hayyu — "The Ever-Living"

*Al-Hayy* is one of the two Names that the Prophet ﷺ highlighted when asked for the greatest name of Allah (Ism al-A'dham).
Allah's life is not like human life — not dependent on food, air, or any sustaining condition.
It is absolute, uncaused, eternal life.

### 3. Al-Qayyum — "The Sustainer of All Existence"

*Al-Qayyum* is from the root *qama* — to stand.
Allah is the one who stands by Himself and upon whom all creation stands.
Ibn Kathir explains: He subsists by Himself and all things subsist through Him.
Every atom in the universe is sustained at every moment by Allah's continuous sustaining act.
Without it, everything would instantly cease to exist.

### 4. La ta'khudhuhu sinatun wala nawm — "Neither drowsiness nor sleep overtakes Him"

This is one of the most powerful descriptions in the Quran.
The word *sinah* is even lighter than sleep — it is the drowsiness that precedes sleep, the involuntary heaviness of the eyelids.
Allah is exempt from both. The One who sustains the universe does not require rest.
He does not have a "night shift" when He is less attentive, does not blink and miss something, does not have moments of reduced capacity.
His attention is absolute and eternal.

### 5. Lahu ma fis-samawati wa ma fil-ard — "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth"

Every atom in every galaxy.
Every creature on every planet. Every thought in every human heart.
All of it belongs to Allah — not in a metaphorical sense, but in the most literal sense of ownership and possession.

### 6. Man dhallathi yashfa'u 'indahu illa bi-idhnih — "Who can intercede except by His permission?"

On the Day of Judgement, intercession (*shafa'ah*) is real — the Prophet ﷺ will intercede for his ummah.
But no one can intercede without Allah's explicit permission. Even the prophets will be waiting for that permission on the Day when *"every soul will be concerned with its own situation."* This verse demolishes any theology that suggests saints, intermediaries, or created beings can approach Allah without His permission.

### 7. Ya'lamu ma bayna aydihim wa ma khalfahum — "He knows what is before them and what is after them"

Allah's knowledge encompasses the past and the future simultaneously.
What happened before creation. What will happen after the Day of Judgement. Everything in between.
There is no timeline that contains His knowledge — His knowledge contains every timeline.

### 8. Wala yuhituna bi-shay'in min 'ilmihi illa bima sha' — "They encompass nothing of His knowledge except what He wills"

Human knowledge is a gift — whatever we know was permitted by Allah for us to know.
The entirety of human science, philosophy, and scholarship is a tiny fragment of the knowledge Allah chose to make accessible.
The rest — the nature of the soul, the mechanics of the afterlife, the full complexity of divine wisdom — is beyond human grasp by divine design.

### 9. Wasi'a kursiyyuhus-samawati wal-ard — "His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth"

The *Kursi* (footstool, throne) of Allah encompasses the entire creation — the seven heavens and the earth and all that is between and beyond them.
The Prophet ﷺ said: *"The seven heavens compared to the Kursi are like a ring thrown into a desert. And the Kursi compared to the Arsh (throne) is like a ring thrown into a desert."* (Ibn Hibban — authenticated).
Creation is incomprehensibly vast; Allah's throne is incomparably more vast.

### 10. Wa la ya'uduhu hifdhuhuma, wa huwal-'Aliyyul-'Adhim — "And their preservation tires Him not, and He is the Most High, the Most Great"

The word *ya'uduhu* is particularly important — it means to find something heavy, burdensome, or tiring.
Sustaining the entire cosmos — every quark, every galaxy, every soul — does not burden Allah in the slightest.
Then the verse closes with two names: *Al-'Aliyy* (the Most High — transcendent above all creation) and *Al-'Adhim* (the Most Great — in a greatness nothing can contain or measure).

## The Five Times the Prophet ﷺ Recommended Ayat al-Kursi

<Callout type="tip">
**After every obligatory prayer**: The Prophet ﷺ said: "Whoever recites Ayat al-Kursi after every obligatory prayer, nothing stands between him and Paradise except death."
(Sunan al-Nasa'i — authenticated by al-Albani)

**Before sleeping**: Along with the last two verses of Surah Al-Baqarah — protection through the night.
**When leaving the home**: Combined with *Bismillah, tawakkaltu 'alallah, la hawla wa la quwwata illa billah* — the Prophet ﷺ said whoever says these will be told: "You are guided, defended, and protected."
**When entering the home**: Reciting it when entering repels poverty and shaytan.
**To protect from shaytan**: The hadith of Abu Hurairah and the jinn captures this — a thief revealed to the Prophet ﷺ that reciting Ayat al-Kursi provides protection from shaytan through the night (Sahih al-Bukhari 5010).
</Callout>

## Memorise Ayat al-Kursi

It is 50 Arabic words. For most Muslims who do not know Arabic, it takes approximately 2–3 weeks of daily repetition to memorise it to a fluent standard.
The reward — protection after every prayer for a lifetime — makes it among the most valuable investments of time in Islamic practice.
Listen to it recited on QuranBookk, read it alongside the Arabic text, and practice daily until it is part of you:

👉 [Read Ayat al-Kursi in Surah Al-Baqarah on QuranBookk](/quran/surahs/english_saheeh/2?verse-number=255)

👉 [Search for Ayat al-Kursi on QuranBookk](/search-quran?q=ayat%20al-kursi)

👉 [Listen to Surah Al-Baqarah audio on QuranBookk](/quran/audio/listen/ar.alafasy/2)

When the Prophet ﷺ says this is the greatest verse in the Quran, he is telling us that no fifty words in any language — in any book ever written — describe the nature of reality more accurately and more completely than these.
Memorise them. Recite them. Reflect on them. They are the foundation of Islamic theology compressed into a single breath.
